name: Stourhead
description: Stourhead is a grand Palladian mansion set amid one of the finest landscape gardens in the world. Created in the 18th century by Henry Hoare II, the garden features a magnificent lake, classical temples, and rare tree collections. The house contains Chippendale furniture and an outstanding library.
shortDescription: World-famous 18th-century landscape garden with a Palladian mansion, classical temples, and a magnificent lake.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Waddesdon Manor
description: Waddesdon Manor is a French Renaissance-style chateau built by Baron Ferdinand de Rothschild in the 1870s. The house contains one of the finest collections of French decorative arts in the world, alongside Old Master paintings and Sèvres porcelain. The grounds include a parterre, aviary, and seasonal art exhibitions.
shortDescription: A Rothschild-built French Renaissance chateau with world-class decorative arts and beautiful gardens.

name: Cliveden
description: Cliveden is a stately home perched above the Thames with formal gardens, woodland, and riverside walks. Once the home of the Astor family and centre of political intrigue, the Italianate mansion is now a luxury hotel while the National Trust manages the spectacular grounds. The parterre, water garden, and Long Garden are among its highlights.
shortDescription: A grand Thames-side estate with Italianate gardens, woodland walks, and a rich political history.

name: Chartwell
description: Chartwell was the beloved family home of Sir Winston Churchill for over 40 years. The rooms remain much as he left them, with personal mementos, maps, and gifts from world leaders. The hillside garden offers sweeping views of the Weald of Kent and includes Churchill's painting studio.
shortDescription: Winston Churchill's cherished family home with personal memorabilia and sweeping Weald views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

One adult and up to three children

name: Sissinghurst Castle Garden
description: Sissinghurst Castle Garden is one of England's most celebrated gardens, created in the 1930s by Vita Sackville-West and Harold Nicolson. The garden is arranged as a series of intimate 'rooms' divided by hedges and walls, including the famous White Garden. The Elizabethan tower offers panoramic views over the Weald.
shortDescription: Vita Sackville-West's iconic garden rooms, including the famous White Garden, set around an Elizabethan tower.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Knole
description: Knole is one of the largest and most important houses in England, set within a medieval deer park. Dating from the 15th century, the house has 365 rooms and contains outstanding collections of furniture, textiles, and paintings. Virginia Woolf immortalised it in her novel Orlando.
shortDescription: A vast 15th-century house with 365 rooms, priceless collections, and a medieval deer park.

name: Scotney Castle
description: Scotney Castle features a romantic medieval moated castle ruin set in a beautiful wooded valley garden. The picturesque Old Castle, dating from the 14th century, was deliberately retained as a garden feature when the new Victorian country house was built above. The estate includes woodland walks and hop gardens.
shortDescription: A romantic moated castle ruin in a wooded valley garden with a Victorian country house above.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Ightham Mote
description: Ightham Mote is a medieval moated manor house dating from around 1320, one of the oldest and most complete in England. The house spans seven centuries of architectural history, from the Great Hall to the Tudor chapel with its painted ceiling. The tranquil garden and surrounding countryside walks add to its charm.
shortDescription: A beautifully preserved medieval moated manor house spanning seven centuries of architectural history.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Emmetts Garden
description: Emmetts Garden is a charming hillside shrub garden, one of the highest in Kent, offering stunning views across the Weald. The informal Edwardian garden features rare trees and shrubs, with spectacular displays of bluebells in spring and vibrant autumn colour. It was originally created by Frederic Lubbock in the late 19th century.
shortDescription: A hillside Edwardian shrub garden with rare trees, bluebells, and panoramic Weald views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: White Cliffs of Dover
description: The White Cliffs of Dover are an iconic landmark of the English coastline, rising to 110 metres above the sea. The chalky grassland is home to rare wildflowers and butterflies, while the clifftop trails offer dramatic views across the English Channel to France. The Fan Bay Deep Shelter, a WWII tunnels network, can also be visited.
shortDescription: England's iconic chalk cliffs with clifftop walks, rare wildlife, and views across to France.

name: Bodiam Castle
description: Bodiam Castle is one of Britain's most picturesque and evocative medieval castles, built in 1385 as both a defence and a comfortable home. The imposing exterior, complete with wide moat and original portcullis, conceals a ruined but atmospheric interior. The surrounding estate offers peaceful riverside walks.
shortDescription: A picture-perfect 14th-century moated castle with a dramatic exterior and atmospheric ruins inside.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Sheffield Park and Garden
description: Sheffield Park is a stunning 120-acre landscape garden laid out in the 18th century by Capability Brown and later transformed with exotic plantings. Four lakes form the centrepiece, surrounded by rare trees and shrubs that produce breathtaking autumn colour displays. The garden is renowned as one of the finest in England for autumn visits.
shortDescription: A magnificent 120-acre landscape garden with four lakes, famed for its spectacular autumn colours.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Bateman's
description: Bateman's is a beautiful 17th-century Jacobean house that was the home of Rudyard Kipling from 1902 until his death in 1936. The rooms remain much as Kipling left them, including the study where he wrote some of his finest works. The garden features a working watermill and the charming Puck's Hill.
shortDescription: Rudyard Kipling's beloved Jacobean home with his study, garden, and working watermill.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Monk's House
description: Monk's House is a modest weatherboarded cottage that was the country retreat of Virginia and Leonard Woolf from 1919 until Leonard's death in 1969. The house contains personal belongings, painted furniture, and the couple's eclectic art collection. The tranquil cottage garden, where Virginia wrote in her lodge, overlooks the Ouse Valley.
shortDescription: Virginia Woolf's country cottage retreat with personal belongings and a tranquil writer's garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Nymans
description: Nymans is one of the great gardens of the Sussex Weald, created over successive generations by the Messel family. The intimate garden rooms are filled with rare plant collections and romantic topiary, while the ruins of the medieval-style house form a dramatic backdrop. The estate also includes woodland and farmland walks.
shortDescription: A celebrated Sussex garden of intimate rooms, rare plants, and romantic house ruins.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Petworth House
description: Petworth House is a magnificent late 17th-century mansion housing one of the finest art collections in the care of the National Trust, including paintings by Turner, Van Dyck, and Reynolds. The 700-acre deer park, landscaped by Capability Brown, is one of the best surviving examples of his work. The Servants' Quarters offer a fascinating glimpse below stairs.
shortDescription: A grand mansion with an outstanding art collection and a 700-acre Capability Brown deer park.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Standen House and Garden
description: Standen is an outstanding Arts and Crafts house designed by Philip Webb and furnished with William Morris textiles and wallpapers. Built in the 1890s, it is one of the finest examples of the Arts and Crafts movement in Britain. The hillside garden offers beautiful views over Ashdown Forest and includes a quarry garden and conservatory.
shortDescription: A superb Arts and Crafts house with William Morris interiors and hillside Ashdown Forest views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Uppark House and Garden
description: Uppark is an elegant 17th-century house set high on the South Downs with panoramic views to the Solent. Meticulously restored after a devastating fire in 1989, the house showcases Georgian interiors, a renowned doll's house collection, and fine paintings. H.G. Wells spent part of his childhood here when his mother was housekeeper.
shortDescription: An elegant South Downs house with Georgian interiors, restored after a dramatic 1989 fire.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Polesden Lacey
description: Polesden Lacey is an Edwardian country house set in 1,400 acres of rolling Surrey Hills countryside. The lavish interiors reflect the glamorous Edwardian hostess Mrs Ronald Greville, who entertained royalty and high society. The walled rose garden, long walk, and views over the North Downs make it a popular year-round destination.
shortDescription: An Edwardian mansion with lavish interiors, a famous rose garden, and sweeping Surrey Hills views.

name: Box Hill
description: Box Hill is a prominent chalk hill in the Surrey Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, named after the ancient box woodland on its steep slopes. The summit offers spectacular views over the Weald and was famously featured in the 2012 Olympic cycling road race. It is a popular destination for walking, cycling, and wildlife spotting.
shortDescription: A chalk hilltop with sweeping Weald views, ancient box woodland, and popular walking trails.

name: Hatchlands Park
description: Hatchlands Park is a Georgian country house built in the 1750s with an interior by Robert Adam, his earliest known decorative scheme. The house contains the Cobbe Collection, the world's largest collection of keyboard instruments associated with famous composers. The 430-acre park offers varied walks through ancient woodland and open parkland.
shortDescription: A Georgian house with Robert Adam interiors, the world's largest keyboard instrument collection, and 430 acres of parkland.

name: Claremont Landscape Garden
description: Claremont Landscape Garden is one of the earliest surviving English landscape gardens, created by some of the greatest names in garden design including Vanbrugh, Bridgeman, Kent, and Capability Brown. The garden features a lake, island, turf amphitheatre, and grotto. It was once the garden of Claremont House, a former royal residence.
shortDescription: One of England's earliest landscape gardens, shaped by Vanbrugh, Kent, and Capability Brown.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Winkworth Arboretum
description: Winkworth Arboretum is a hillside woodland planted with over 1,000 different shrubs and trees from around the world. Created by Dr Wilfrid Fox in the early 20th century, the arboretum is renowned for its spectacular bluebell carpet in spring and vivid autumn foliage reflected in the lake. It covers 112 acres of the Surrey Hills.
shortDescription: A hillside arboretum with over 1,000 tree species, famous for spring bluebells and autumn colour.

name: The Vyne
description: The Vyne is a Tudor mansion built in the early 16th century for Lord Sandys, Lord Chamberlain to Henry VIII. It was the first country house in England to have a classical portico, added in the 1650s. The house contains fine Tudor chapel stained glass, a Palladian staircase, and the atmospheric Stone Gallery.
shortDescription: A Tudor mansion with England's first classical portico, fine stained glass, and a Palladian staircase.

name: Mottisfont
description: Mottisfont is a country house with medieval priory origins, set in riverside grounds along the Test. It is home to the National Collection of old-fashioned roses, which are at their peak in June. The house features a Rex Whistler drawing room and a contemporary art gallery, while the walled garden and surrounding estate offer year-round interest.
shortDescription: A priory-turned-country house with the National Collection of old roses and Rex Whistler interiors.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hinton Ampner
description: Hinton Ampner is an elegant country house remodelled in the mid-20th century by Ralph Dutton, the last Lord Sherborne. It showcases his superb collection of English furniture, Italian paintings, and hard-stone ornaments. The terraced garden, designed by Dutton himself, is considered one of the great gardens of the 20th century with sweeping views over the Hampshire countryside.
shortDescription: An elegant country house with fine collections and a celebrated 20th-century terraced garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Winchester City Mill
description: Winchester City Mill is a working watermill straddling the River Itchen in the heart of Winchester. There has been a mill on this site since at least the Domesday Book of 1086. Restored to full working order, visitors can see flour being milled by waterpower and learn about 1,000 years of milling history.
shortDescription: A restored working watermill in the heart of Winchester with over 1,000 years of milling history.

name: Ham House and Garden
description: Ham House is one of Europe's best-preserved 17th-century houses, built in 1610 and lavishly expanded by the Duke and Duchess of Lauderdale. The house retains much of its original furniture, textiles, and paintings, including many Stuart-era pieces. The formal garden has been restored to its 17th-century design with a cherry garden, wilderness, and kitchen garden.
shortDescription: One of Europe's best-preserved Stuart-era houses with original furnishings and a restored 17th-century garden.

name: Osterley Park and House
description: Osterley Park is one of the last great country estates in London, a grand Neo-classical villa redesigned by Robert Adam in the 1760s for the Child banking family. The lavish interiors are among Adam's finest, with Etruscan dressing rooms and a state bed. The surrounding parkland includes ancient oak avenues, a lake, and working farm.
shortDescription: A grand Robert Adam Neo-classical villa with lavish interiors set in one of London's last great estates.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Red House
description: Red House was designed by Philip Webb and William Morris in 1859 as Morris's family home and is regarded as the birthplace of the Arts and Crafts movement. The house features original Morris wall paintings, stained glass by Edward Burne-Jones, and innovative Gothic Revival architecture. The intimate garden was designed by Morris as a series of outdoor rooms.
shortDescription: William Morris's pioneering Arts and Crafts home with original wall paintings and Burne-Jones glass.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Fenton House
description: Fenton House is a charming 17th-century merchant's house in Hampstead, one of the oldest and largest houses in the area. It houses the Benton Fletcher Collection of early keyboard instruments and fine porcelain, alongside paintings and Georgian furniture. The walled garden is a hidden gem with an orchard, kitchen garden, and formal terraces.
shortDescription: A 17th-century Hampstead merchant's house with early keyboard instruments and a hidden walled garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: 2 Willow Road
description: 2 Willow Road is a groundbreaking Modernist home designed and built by architect Erno Goldfinger in 1939 for his own family. The house showcases his innovative approach to space, light, and materials, and contains the Goldfingers' impressive collection of modern art by Henry Moore, Max Ernst, and Bridget Riley. It is the National Trust's first Modernist property.
shortDescription: Erno Goldfinger's pioneering 1939 Modernist home in Hampstead, the National Trust's first of its kind.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Basildon Park
description: Basildon Park is a dramatic Palladian mansion built in the 1770s for Sir Francis Sykes, set in 400 acres of parkland and woodland overlooking the Thames Valley. The house was rescued from dereliction in the 1950s by Lord and Lady Iliffe and features beautifully restored interiors. It has been used as a filming location for Downton Abbey and Pride and Prejudice.
shortDescription: A rescued Palladian mansion overlooking the Thames Valley, used as a Downton Abbey filming location.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Greys Court
description: Greys Court is a picturesque Tudor mansion set amid medieval ruins, including a fortified tower and the remains of a 14th-century courtyard. The intimate walled gardens include a rose garden, kitchen garden, and the Archbishop's Maze, inspired by the journey of reconciliation. The surrounding estate offers walks through ancient woodland and Chiltern countryside.
shortDescription: A picturesque Tudor mansion with medieval ruins, a walled garden, and the Archbishop's Maze.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Stowe
description: Stowe is one of the most significant landscape gardens in Europe, with over 40 temples and monuments set across 250 acres of sweeping lawns, lakes, and valleys. Designed by the greatest landscape architects of the 18th century including Bridgeman, Kent, and Capability Brown, it is a masterpiece of the English Landscape movement. The gardens inspired countless imitations worldwide.
shortDescription: One of Europe's finest landscape gardens with over 40 temples across 250 acres of sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Claydon House
description: Claydon House is an 18th-century country house renowned for its extraordinary Rococo interiors, including the unique Chinese Room with intricate carvings. The house has a long connection with Florence Nightingale, whose sister married into the Verney family, and a room is dedicated to her memory. The surrounding parkland offers peaceful walks with views across the Vale of Aylesbury.
shortDescription: An 18th-century house with extraordinary Rococo carvings and strong connections to Florence Nightingale.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Anglesey Abbey
description: Anglesey Abbey is a Jacobean-style house built on the site of a 12th-century Augustinian priory, transformed by the 1st Lord Fairhaven into a treasure house. The house contains an outstanding collection of paintings, furniture, and clocks. The 114-acre garden is famous for its winter garden, one of the finest in England, along with its dahlia garden and sweeping avenues.
shortDescription: A priory-turned-treasure house with outstanding collections and one of England's finest winter gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wimpole Estate
description: Wimpole Estate is Cambridgeshire's most impressive country house, an 18th-century mansion set in 3,000 acres of parkland, farmland, and gardens designed by Capability Brown. The house features grand interiors including a spectacular library and Baroque chapel. The estate includes a working Home Farm with rare breed animals, particularly popular with families.
shortDescription: Cambridgeshire's grandest country house with a Capability Brown park and a working rare breeds farm.

name: Wicken Fen
description: Wicken Fen is one of Britain's oldest nature reserves and one of Europe's most important wetlands. This ancient fenland, the National Trust's first nature reserve acquired in 1899, supports over 9,000 species including rare butterflies, birds, and plants. The reserve offers boardwalk trails, a windpump, and boat trips, and is part of the Wicken Fen Vision to create a 53-square-kilometre nature reserve.
shortDescription: One of Britain's oldest nature reserves, a precious fenland wetland supporting over 9,000 species.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Blickling Estate
description: Blickling Estate centres on a magnificent Jacobean redbrick mansion, one of England's great houses, reputed birthplace of Anne Boleyn. The house contains a spectacular long gallery, an outstanding library of rare books, and fine tapestries. The formal garden features parterre beds, an orangery, and a secret garden, while the wider estate offers miles of woodland and lakeside walks.
shortDescription: A magnificent Jacobean mansion, reputed birthplace of Anne Boleyn, with a spectacular library and formal gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Felbrigg Hall
description: Felbrigg Hall is one of the grandest 17th-century houses in Norfolk, with a Jacobean exterior and elegant Georgian interiors. The house contains an outstanding collection of 18th-century Grand Tour paintings and an original Gothic library. The walled garden has been restored as a working kitchen garden and dovecote, while the 520-acre wooded parkland offers peaceful walks just two miles from the coast.
shortDescription: A grand 17th-century Norfolk house with Grand Tour paintings, a Gothic library, and wooded parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Oxburgh Hall
description: Oxburgh Hall is a stunning moated manor house built by the Bedingfeld family in 1482, who have lived here for over 500 years. The imposing Tudor gatehouse is one of the finest in England, and the house contains needlework by Mary, Queen of Scots. After a major conservation project, the house showcases both its medieval origins and later alterations.
shortDescription: A moated Tudor manor with a magnificent gatehouse and needlework by Mary, Queen of Scots.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Sheringham Park
description: Sheringham Park is a landscape park designed by Humphry Repton, who considered it his finest work. The park is famous for its spectacular display of rhododendrons and azaleas in late spring, with viewing towers offering panoramic views to the North Norfolk coast. The estate includes coastal walks, woodland trails, and a working farm.
shortDescription: Humphry Repton's self-proclaimed finest landscape park, famous for spectacular rhododendron displays.

name: Ickworth
description: Ickworth is one of the most unusual country houses in England, with an enormous Italianate rotunda at its centre flanked by curving wings. Built by the eccentric 4th Earl of Bristol in the late 18th century, the house contains outstanding collections of Georgian silver, Regency furniture, and Old Master paintings. The 1,800-acre parkland, partly designed by Capability Brown, includes an Italianate garden, vineyard walk, and woodland play area.
shortDescription: An extraordinary Italianate rotunda house with Georgian silver and 1,800 acres of Capability Brown parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Flatford
description: Flatford is the heart of Constable Country, where John Constable painted some of his most famous works including The Hay Wain and Flatford Mill. The hamlet includes Flatford Mill, Bridge Cottage, and Willy Lott's House, all recognisable from Constable's paintings. The surrounding Dedham Vale Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty offers beautiful riverside walks along the Stour.
shortDescription: The heart of Constable Country with the landscapes that inspired The Hay Wain and other masterpieces.

name: Lavenham Guildhall
description: Lavenham Guildhall is a stunning timber-framed Tudor building dating from the 1520s, originally the meeting hall of the Guild of Corpus Christi. The building has served as a town hall, prison, workhouse, and almshouse over the centuries. Today it houses exhibitions on local history, the medieval wool trade, and farming, set in one of England's best-preserved medieval villages.
shortDescription: A magnificent timber-framed Tudor guildhall in one of England's finest medieval villages.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family One Adult (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Belton House
description: Belton House is a grand country house built in 1685-88 for Sir John Brownlow in the graceful style of the English Baroque. The interiors feature exquisite wood carvings, fine porcelain, and silverware collections spanning three centuries. Set within 1,300 acres of parkland and formal gardens, the estate includes an orangery, Italian and Dutch gardens, and an adventure playground.
shortDescription: An elegant late-17th-century country house set in magnificent formal gardens and vast parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Tattershall Castle
description: Tattershall Castle is a dramatic red-brick tower keep built in the 1430s by Ralph Cromwell, Lord Treasurer of England. Rising six storeys and over 30 metres high, it is one of the finest examples of medieval brick building in England. The castle was rescued from demolition by Lord Curzon in 1911, who donated it to the National Trust along with its impressive Gothic fireplaces.
shortDescription: A towering medieval red-brick castle keep built for a Lord Treasurer of England.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Woolsthorpe Manor
description: Woolsthorpe Manor is the birthplace of Sir Isaac Newton, where he was born on Christmas Day 1642 and later developed his theories of light, gravity, and calculus. The modest 17th-century farmhouse still contains the room where Newton conducted his famous prism experiments. In the garden stands a descendant of the apple tree said to have inspired his theory of gravity.
shortDescription: The birthplace of Sir Isaac Newton, where he developed his groundbreaking scientific theories.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Kedleston Hall
description: Kedleston Hall is a magnificent Palladian mansion designed by Robert Adam in the 1760s for the Curzon family. The grand marble hall and state rooms contain outstanding collections of paintings, furniture, and Old Master drawings. The 800-acre landscaped park features a serpentine lake, a medieval church, and long walks through ancient woodland and pleasure grounds.
shortDescription: A grand Palladian mansion by Robert Adam set in 800 acres of sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Calke Abbey
description: Calke Abbey is a baroque mansion known as 'the house that time forgot,' filled with collections amassed by the reclusive Harpur-Crewe family over centuries. The unstored rooms reveal peeling wallpaper, cluttered collections, and faded grandeur deliberately preserved by the National Trust. The surrounding 600-acre parkland is a National Nature Reserve with ancient trees and rare wildlife.
shortDescription: A baroque mansion frozen in time, filled with eccentric collections and surrounded by a nature reserve.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardwick Hall
description: Hardwick Hall is an Elizabethan masterpiece built in the 1590s by Bess of Hardwick, one of the richest women in England. Famous for its vast windows — inspiring the rhyme 'Hardwick Hall, more glass than wall' — the house contains outstanding tapestries and embroideries. The estate includes formal gardens, an Elizabethan herb garden, and the ruins of the adjacent Old Hall.
shortDescription: A spectacular Elizabethan prodigy house famous for its vast windows and outstanding tapestries.

name: Sudbury Hall
description: Sudbury Hall is a 17th-century country house renowned for its lavish interiors, including intricate plasterwork ceilings, elaborate wood carvings by Grinling Gibbons, and a magnificent Great Staircase. The house also hosts the National Trust Museum of Childhood, exploring what it was like to grow up in different periods of history. The grounds feature a lakeside walk and walled garden.
shortDescription: A 17th-century house with stunning decorative interiors and the National Trust Museum of Childhood.

name: Canons Ashby
description: Canons Ashby is an Elizabethan manor house built using the remains of an Augustinian priory, home to the Dryden family for over 450 years. The intimate interiors feature Jacobean plasterwork, Elizabethan wall paintings, and a fine collection of period furniture. The formal gardens include an ancient terraced layout with topiary yews and a medieval priory church.
shortDescription: An intimate Elizabethan manor house built from a medieval priory with beautiful terraced gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Lyveden
description: Lyveden is an unfinished Elizabethan lodge built by Sir Thomas Tresham to express his Catholic faith through symbolic architecture. The roofless shell, with its elaborate friezes of religious symbols, stands as a remarkable monument to recusant devotion. The surrounding garden preserves rare Elizabethan earthworks including moated canals, terraces, and an orchard.
shortDescription: An enigmatic unfinished Elizabethan lodge built as a secret symbol of Catholic faith.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Baddesley Clinton
description: Baddesley Clinton is a romantic medieval moated manor house dating from the 15th century, hidden away in the Forest of Arden. The house is notable for its priest holes, where Catholic priests were concealed during the Reformation, and its atmospheric interiors filled with heraldic glass and period furnishings. The grounds include a tranquil lakeside walk, walled garden, and nature trail.
shortDescription: A romantic medieval moated manor house with priest holes hidden in the Forest of Arden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Charlecote Park
description: Charlecote Park is an imposing Elizabethan house set in a deer park landscaped by Capability Brown on the banks of the River Avon. The house has been home to the Lucy family since 1247, and legend claims the young William Shakespeare was caught poaching deer here. The Victorian interiors are richly decorated with family treasures, and the park still has herds of fallow and red deer.
shortDescription: An Elizabethan mansion in a Capability Brown deer park, linked to the young Shakespeare.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Coughton Court
description: Coughton Court is a magnificent Tudor house that has been home to the Throckmorton family since 1409. The imposing gatehouse and half-timbered wings house one of the finest collections of Catholic recusant history in England, with direct connections to the Gunpowder Plot. The gardens include a walled garden, riverside walk, and a stunning display garden created by Christina Williams.
shortDescription: A Tudor house with deep Gunpowder Plot connections, home to the same family for over 600 years.

name: Packwood House
description: Packwood House is a beautifully restored Tudor house surrounded by one of the most famous topiary gardens in England. The Yew Garden, said to represent the Sermon on the Mount, features towering yew trees shaped over centuries. The interiors were lovingly furnished in the 1920s-30s by Baron Ash with tapestries, needlework, and fine furniture to create an idealised Tudor home.
shortDescription: A Tudor house with one of England's most renowned topiary gardens representing the Sermon on the Mount.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Upton House and Gardens
description: Upton House is a late-17th-century country house remodelled in the 1920s-30s by the 2nd Viscount Bearsted to display his world-class art collection. The house contains masterpieces by El Greco, Canaletto, Stubbs, and Hogarth, alongside Brussels tapestries and Chelsea porcelain. The terraced gardens cascade dramatically down a hidden valley to a mirror pool, with a 1930s bog garden and herbaceous borders.
shortDescription: A country house with a world-class art collection and dramatic terraced gardens in a hidden valley.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Attingham Park
description: Attingham Park is an elegant late-18th-century mansion designed by George Steuart for the 1st Baron Berwick, set in 200 acres of parkland landscaped by Humphry Repton. The grand Neoclassical interiors include a picture gallery with an early cast-iron and glass roof designed by John Nash. The walled garden has been restored as a productive kitchen garden, and the deer park is home to a herd of fallow deer.
shortDescription: A Neoclassical mansion in Repton-designed parkland with a Nash picture gallery and deer park.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Berrington Hall
description: Berrington Hall is an elegant Georgian mansion designed by Henry Holland in the 1770s, set within a Capability Brown landscape of sweeping parkland and a serpentine lake. The refined interiors feature delicate plasterwork ceilings, a Regency laundry, and a collection of paintings and furniture. The walled garden is being restored to its productive heritage, and the parkland offers walks with views to the Welsh mountains.
shortDescription: An elegant Georgian mansion by Henry Holland in a Capability Brown parkland setting.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Croft Castle
description: Croft Castle has been home to the Croft family for nearly 1,000 years, with the medieval castle walls enclosing elegant Georgian interiors. The estate includes a pre-Norman church, an Iron Age hill fort at Croft Ambrey, and an avenue of 350-year-old sweet chestnuts. The parkland and woodland walks offer far-reaching views across the Welsh Marches.
shortDescription: A castle held by the same family for nearly 1,000 years, with ancient woodland and an Iron Age hill fort.

name: Brockhampton Estate
description: Brockhampton Estate centres on a picturesque medieval moated manor house with a rare detached half-timbered gatehouse dating from the late 15th century. The surrounding 1,700-acre estate includes traditional orchards, ancient woodland, and farmland in a quiet Herefordshire valley. Seasonal trails wind through bluebell woods and along streams, making it a haven for wildlife and walkers alike.
shortDescription: A medieval moated manor house with a rare timber gatehouse set in a tranquil Herefordshire estate.

name: Croome
description: Croome is a Georgian mansion whose landscape was the first major commission for the young Lancelot 'Capability' Brown, and whose interiors were designed by Robert Adam. The wartime story of RAF Defford, a secret radar research base on the estate, adds another dimension to the property. The restored parkland features a lake, church, grotto, and panoramic hilltop viewpoint.
shortDescription: Capability Brown's first landscape commission with Robert Adam interiors and a secret wartime past.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hanbury Hall
description: Hanbury Hall is a William and Mary-style red-brick house built in 1701 for the wealthy lawyer Thomas Vernon. The highlight is the stunning painted staircase and ceiling by Sir James Thornhill, rivalling his work at St Paul's Cathedral and Greenwich. The restored 18th-century formal gardens include a sunken parterre, fruit garden, bowling green, and an ice house.
shortDescription: A Queen Anne mansion famous for its Thornhill-painted staircase and restored 18th-century gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hidcote
description: Hidcote is one of England's great Arts and Crafts gardens, created in the early 20th century by the American-born horticulturalist Lawrence Johnston. The garden is a masterful sequence of outdoor 'rooms,' each with a distinct character, separated by tall hedges and walls. Rare and unusual plants from Johnston's global expeditions fill the borders, and the design has influenced gardens worldwide.
shortDescription: One of England's most influential Arts and Crafts gardens, designed as a series of outdoor rooms.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Dyrham Park
description: Dyrham Park is a baroque country house built in the 1690s for William Blathwayt, Secretary of State to William III. The house is renowned for its Dutch-influenced interiors filled with Delftware, paintings, and period furniture. The 270-acre deer park offers walks with panoramic views over the surrounding countryside, while the Victorian garden features terraces and a restored greenhouse.
shortDescription: A baroque mansion with Dutch-influenced interiors set in an ancient deer park near Bath.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Chedworth Roman Villa
description: Chedworth Roman Villa is one of the largest and best-preserved Romano-British villas in England, discovered by a gamekeeper in 1864. The remains include outstanding mosaic floors, two bath suites, a water shrine, and underfloor heating systems dating from the 2nd to 4th centuries AD. A modern conservation shelter protects the mosaics while allowing visitors to walk among the ancient rooms.
shortDescription: One of Britain's finest Roman villas with outstanding mosaic floors in a Cotswold woodland setting.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Newark Park
description: Newark Park is a Tudor hunting lodge dramatically sited on the Cotswold escarpment, later converted into a country house with Georgian and Victorian additions. The house was rescued from near-dereliction in the 1970s by the architect and craftsman Michael Claydon, whose personal collection fills the rooms. The garden and estate offer spectacular views over the Ozleworth Valley towards the Severn Estuary and Wales.
shortDescription: A Tudor hunting lodge perched on the Cotswold escarpment with spectacular valley views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Tyntesfield
description: Tyntesfield is a spectacular Victorian Gothic Revival mansion built for the Gibbs family, who made their fortune from Peruvian guano. The house retains virtually all of its original contents, including Gothic furniture, stained glass, and a private chapel rivalling a small cathedral. The 540-acre estate features terraced gardens, a kitchen garden, and extensive parkland with far-reaching views across North Somerset.
shortDescription: A lavish Victorian Gothic mansion with original contents and a chapel of cathedral proportions.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Montacute House
description: Montacute House is one of the finest Elizabethan mansions in England, built in the 1590s from local Ham Hill stone with a magnificent Long Gallery — the longest in the country. The house displays an outstanding collection of Tudor and Elizabethan portraits on loan from the National Portrait Gallery. The formal gardens include mixed borders, a raised walkway, and an 18th-century landscape park.
shortDescription: A masterpiece of Elizabethan architecture with England's longest Long Gallery and Tudor portraits.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Dunster Castle
description: Dunster Castle is a dramatically sited medieval fortress perched on a steep hill overlooking the Bristol Channel, remodelled in the 19th century as a comfortable country home. The castle has been occupied for over 1,000 years, with a gatehouse, towers, and interiors spanning the medieval, Jacobean, and Victorian periods. The subtropical terraced gardens feature rare plants, a working watermill, and views to Exmoor.
shortDescription: A medieval hilltop castle with subtropical gardens overlooking the Bristol Channel and Exmoor.

name: Prior Park Landscape Garden
description: Prior Park Landscape Garden is an intimate 18th-century landscape garden created by the entrepreneur Ralph Allen with advice from Alexander Pope and Capability Brown. The centrepiece is one of only four Palladian bridges in the world, framing views across the sweeping valley to the city of Bath. The garden features a serpentine lake, wilderness walk, and a restored 18th-century rock gate and cascade.
shortDescription: An 18th-century landscape garden with a rare Palladian bridge and sweeping views of Bath.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Glastonbury Tor
description: Glastonbury Tor is an iconic conical hill rising 158 metres above the Somerset Levels, crowned by the roofless tower of the medieval St Michael's Church. Steeped in legend and myth — linked to King Arthur, the Holy Grail, and the Isle of Avalon — the Tor has been a place of spiritual significance for thousands of years. The summit offers breathtaking 360-degree views across Somerset to the Mendip Hills, Quantocks, and beyond.
shortDescription: An iconic hilltop tower steeped in Arthurian legend with panoramic views across the Somerset Levels.

name: Lytes Cary Manor
description: Lytes Cary Manor is a charming medieval manor house that was home to the Lyte family for 500 years, including the Elizabethan botanist Henry Lyte who wrote the influential herbal 'Niewe Herball.' The intimate rooms contain period furnishings and a collection of Lyte's botanical works. The Arts and Crafts-style gardens, created in the 20th century, feature herbaceous borders, topiary, and a tranquil orchard.
shortDescription: A medieval manor house linked to the Elizabethan botanist Henry Lyte, with Arts and Crafts gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Kingston Lacy
description: Kingston Lacy is a grand 17th-century country house built for the Bankes family after they lost Corfe Castle during the English Civil War. The lavish interiors contain an outstanding art collection, including works by Rubens, Titian, and Van Dyck, as well as an Egyptian obelisk in the grounds. The 8,500-acre estate includes ancient woodland, Iron Age hill forts, and the famous Badbury Rings.
shortDescription: A grand country house with masterpieces by Rubens and Titian, set in an 8,500-acre estate.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Corfe Castle
description: Corfe Castle is one of Britain's most iconic ruins, a dramatic Norman fortress perched on a natural chalk hill in the Purbeck Hills. The castle was built by William the Conqueror and later besieged and deliberately demolished by Parliamentarian forces during the English Civil War. The ruins offer commanding views over the surrounding Isle of Purbeck and the village of Corfe Castle below.
shortDescription: An iconic ruined Norman fortress dramatically perched on a chalk hill in the Purbeck Hills.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Brownsea Island
description: Brownsea Island is a 500-acre island in the middle of Poole Harbour, famous as the birthplace of the Scout and Guide movements, where Robert Baden-Powell held his first experimental camp in 1907. The island is a haven for rare red squirrels and a diverse range of wading birds in its saltwater lagoon. Visitors reach the island by boat and can explore woodland trails, beaches, and the Dorset Wildlife Trust nature reserve.
shortDescription: An island nature reserve in Poole Harbour, birthplace of the Scouting movement and home to red squirrels.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Badbury Rings
description: Badbury Rings is a spectacular Iron Age hill fort with three concentric rings of massive earthwork ramparts and ditches, thought to date from around 800 BC. The site has legendary associations with King Arthur's Battle of Mount Badon and sits at the junction of two Roman roads. The surrounding grasslands and nearby tree-lined avenue of beech trees form a rich habitat for butterflies, wildflowers, and birdsong.
shortDescription: A triple-ringed Iron Age hill fort with Arthurian legend links and rich wildflower grasslands.

name: Cerne Giant
description: The Cerne Giant is a 55-metre-tall figure of a naked man wielding a club, cut into the chalk hillside above the village of Cerne Abbas. Recent scientific analysis has dated the figure to the late Saxon period, around the 10th century, though its precise origins and meaning remain debated. The figure is best viewed from a designated viewpoint on the opposite hill, and the surrounding chalk downland is rich in wildflowers and butterflies.
shortDescription: A mysterious 55-metre chalk hill figure of ancient origin overlooking the village of Cerne Abbas.

name: Clouds Hill
description: Clouds Hill is the tiny isolated cottage that was the rural retreat of T. E. Lawrence — Lawrence of Arabia — where he sought solitude after his wartime adventures. The spartan rooms remain much as Lawrence left them, filled with his books, gramophone, and personal effects. Lawrence died in a motorcycle accident nearby in 1935, and the cottage offers a uniquely intimate portrait of one of the 20th century's most enigmatic figures.
shortDescription: The tiny rural retreat of T. E. Lawrence, preserved as he left it before his tragic death.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Eggardon Hill
description: Eggardon Hill is a dramatic Iron Age hill fort crowning one of the highest points in west Dorset, with triple ramparts and deep ditches still clearly defined. The exposed hilltop offers extraordinary panoramic views stretching from the Dorset coastline to the hills of Devon and Somerset. The chalk grassland is alive with wildflowers including orchids, and the site is frequented by skylarks, buzzards, and peregrine falcons.
shortDescription: A dramatic Iron Age hill fort on a west Dorset hilltop with sweeping views to the coast and beyond.

name: Fontmell and Melbury Downs
description: Fontmell and Melbury Downs are sweeping chalk downlands on the edge of Cranborne Chase, forming one of the finest stretches of unimproved chalk grassland in Dorset. The steep escarpment and rolling hilltops support a remarkable diversity of wildflowers, butterflies — including chalkhill blue and Adonis blue — and ground-nesting birds. Circular walks offer panoramic views across the Blackmore Vale and surrounding Dorset countryside.
shortDescription: Pristine chalk downlands rich in rare butterflies and wildflowers with panoramic Blackmore Vale views.

name: Golden Cap
description: Golden Cap is the highest point on the south coast of England at 191 metres, named for the distinctive golden-orange sandstone cap rock that crowns its summit. Part of the Jurassic Coast World Heritage Site, the cliffs reveal 185 million years of geological history and are rich in fossils. The surrounding estate includes wildflower meadows, ancient woodlands, hidden valleys, and secluded beaches accessible by footpath.
shortDescription: The highest cliff on England's south coast, a golden-topped landmark on the Jurassic Coast.

name: Hambledon Hill
description: Hambledon Hill is a prominent chalk hill crowned by a massive Neolithic causewayed enclosure and Iron Age hill fort, with earthworks spanning over 5,000 years of human activity. The site saw dramatic conflict during the English Civil War when Clubmen — local people opposing both sides — were routed by Oliver Cromwell's forces. The steep slopes support species-rich chalk grassland with orchids, butterflies, and commanding views over the Blackmore Vale.
shortDescription: A Neolithic and Iron Age hilltop site with Civil War history and species-rich chalk grasslands.

name: Hardy Monument
description: The Hardy Monument is a tall octagonal tower erected in 1844 on Black Down hill to commemorate Vice-Admiral Sir Thomas Masterman Hardy, flag captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar. Standing at 22 metres tall on one of the highest points in south Dorset, it commands exceptional panoramic views over the coast, Portland, Chesil Beach, and the Dorset countryside. The surrounding heathland is managed for wildlife and offers bracing walks.
shortDescription: A hilltop monument to Nelson's flag captain at Trafalgar, commanding sweeping Dorset coastal views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardy's Cottage
description: Hardy's Cottage is the small cob and thatch cottage in Higher Bockhampton where the novelist and poet Thomas Hardy was born in 1840 and wrote his early novels including 'Under the Greenwood Tree' and 'Far from the Madding Crowd.' The cottage was built by Hardy's great-grandfather and retains its modest rural character. The surrounding woodland and heath inspired many settings in Hardy's Wessex novels.
shortDescription: The thatched birthplace of Thomas Hardy, where he wrote his first published novels.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardy's House, Max Gate
description: Max Gate is the Victorian house designed by Thomas Hardy himself in 1885, where he lived until his death in 1928 and wrote many of his greatest works including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles' and 'Jude the Obscure.' The modest but comfortable house reflects Hardy's architectural training and personal taste. The garden, which Hardy planted with extensive tree cover for privacy, has been replanted with species he would have known.
shortDescription: The house Thomas Hardy designed for himself, where he wrote his greatest novels and poetry.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hive Beach
description: Hive Beach is a dramatic shingle beach at Burton Bradstock on the Jurassic Coast, backed by towering golden sandstone cliffs that glow in the sunlight. The beach is popular for swimming, fossil hunting, and fishing, with views east along Chesil Beach towards Portland. The clifftop café is well known locally, and coastal footpaths lead to neighbouring bays and the Golden Cap estate.
shortDescription: A dramatic Jurassic Coast shingle beach beneath golden sandstone cliffs at Burton Bradstock.

name: Hod Hill
description: Hod Hill is one of the largest Iron Age hillforts in Britain, with dramatic ramparts enclosing over 50 acres of Dorset downland. The Romans later built a fort in one corner after conquering the site in AD 43. Today the hill offers panoramic views across the Stour Valley and is rich in wildflowers and butterflies.
shortDescription: Massive Iron Age hillfort with Roman remains and sweeping views across the Stour Valley.

name: Lambert's and Coney's Castle
description: Lambert's Castle and Coney's Castle are neighbouring Iron Age hillforts set in the rolling hills of west Dorset near the Devon border. The sites are cloaked in woodland and heathland, providing habitat for rare butterflies and birds. Superb views extend south to the Jurassic Coast and north across the Marshwood Vale.
shortDescription: Twin Iron Age hillforts in west Dorset with heathland, woodland and far-reaching coastal views.

name: Lewesdon Hill
description: Lewesdon Hill is the highest point in Dorset at 279 metres, crowned by ancient beech trees and surrounded by species-rich grassland. A short but steep walk leads to the summit, which offers panoramic views in every direction. The hill is a haven for wildlife including dormice and a wide range of woodland birds.
shortDescription: Dorset's highest hill, offering panoramic views from a beech-crowned summit.

name: Pilsdon Pen
description: Pilsdon Pen is an Iron Age hillfort and one of the highest points in Dorset, rising to 277 metres. The exposed summit grassland supports a rich variety of wildflowers and butterflies. From the top, walkers enjoy extensive views over the Marshwood Vale, the Jurassic Coast and into neighbouring Devon and Somerset.
shortDescription: High Iron Age hillfort with wildflower-rich grassland and views to the Jurassic Coast.

name: Ringstead Bay
description: Ringstead Bay is a quiet shingle and sand beach on the Jurassic Coast between Weymouth and Lulworth. The sheltered bay is backed by wildflower-rich cliffs and offers views across to the Isle of Portland. It is a popular spot for swimming, fossil hunting and coastal walks along the South West Coast Path.
shortDescription: Sheltered Jurassic Coast bay with shingle beach, wildflower cliffs and fossil hunting.

name: Spyway
description: Spyway is a dramatic Dorset countryside property set on the Purbeck ridge above the village of Langton Matravers. The land includes ancient strip lynchets, stone quarries and rich limestone grassland. Walkers can enjoy stunning views over the coast to Dancing Ledge and beyond to the Isle of Wight.
shortDescription: Purbeck ridge countryside with ancient strip lynchets and dramatic coastal views to Dancing Ledge.

name: Studland Bay
description: Studland Bay is a four-mile sweep of golden sandy beach on the Dorset coast, stretching from Shell Bay to Old Harry Rocks. The beach is backed by a nationally important nature reserve of heathland, sand dunes and freshwater bog, home to all six native reptile species. The area offers excellent swimming, kayaking and walking with views across Poole Harbour.
shortDescription: Four miles of golden sand backed by rare heathland, with views to Old Harry Rocks.

name: White Mill
description: White Mill is a charming 18th-century corn mill on the River Stour near Sturminster Marshall in Dorset. The mill retains its original wooden machinery and still grinds flour on open days. Set in a peaceful riverside meadow, it provides a window into the area's agricultural heritage.
shortDescription: Working 18th-century corn mill on the River Stour with original wooden machinery.

name: Lanhydrock
description: Lanhydrock is a magnificent late Victorian country house set in extensive grounds above the River Fowey near Bodmin. The house, largely rebuilt after a fire in 1881, offers a vivid picture of life in a wealthy Victorian household from the grand Long Gallery to the sprawling kitchens. The surrounding estate includes formal gardens, ancient parkland and woodland walks.
shortDescription: Grand Victorian country house near Bodmin with extensive gardens and riverside parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
One adult and up to 3 children
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Cotehele
description: Cotehele is a remarkably complete medieval house on the steep wooded banks of the River Tamar. Built between 1485 and 1627 by the Edgcumbe family, its rooms are hung with tapestries and decorated with original armour and furniture. The estate includes terraced gardens, a restored quay with a sailing barge, and the valley garden at Cotehele Mill.
shortDescription: Medieval manor house on the River Tamar with tapestries, terraced gardens and a historic quay.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Trelissick Garden
description: Trelissick Garden is a beautiful and varied garden occupying a commanding position above the Fal estuary near Truro. Tender and exotic plants thrive in the mild Cornish climate, while the surrounding parkland and woodland walks offer sweeping views of the river. A gallery showcases the work of Cornish artists, and the King Harry Ferry operates nearby.
shortDescription: Subtropical garden above the Fal estuary with exotic planting and sweeping river views.

name: Glendurgan Garden
description: Glendurgan Garden is a subtropical valley garden running down to the tiny fishing hamlet of Durgan on the Helford River. Created in the 1820s by the Fox family, the garden features a famous laurel maze, giant tree ferns and a stunning collection of camellias and magnolias. Three valleys of exotic planting create sheltered microclimates perfect for tender species.
shortDescription: Subtropical valley garden with a famous laurel maze tumbling down to the Helford River.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Trerice
description: Trerice is an intimate Elizabethan manor house hidden in a quiet valley near Newquay. Built in 1573 by Sir John Arundell, the house retains its original ornate plasterwork ceilings and a magnificent hall window of 576 panes. The surrounding garden includes an orchard of Cornish apple varieties and a sunny knot garden.
shortDescription: Hidden Elizabethan manor near Newquay with ornate plasterwork and a Cornish apple orchard.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Godolphin
description: Godolphin is a romantic former Tudor and Stuart estate near Helston, once home to the powerful Godolphin family who made their fortune from local tin mining. The intimate house has medieval origins and retains rare Elizabethan features. The garden, once lost to nature, has been carefully reclaimed and includes the King's Garden and the celebrated Side Garden.
shortDescription: Romantic Tudor estate near Helston with reclaimed gardens and Cornish mining heritage.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Levant Mine and Beam Engine
description: Levant Mine is a restored 19th-century tin and copper mine perched on dramatic cliffs near St Just in Cornwall's far west. The site houses the oldest beam engine in Cornwall, still powered by steam on regular steaming days. The mine is part of the Cornwall and West Devon Mining Landscape UNESCO World Heritage Site and offers spectacular Atlantic views.
shortDescription: Restored clifftop tin mine with the oldest working beam engine in Cornwall.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Saltram
description: Saltram is a magnificent George II mansion set in a landscaped parkland on the outskirts of Plymouth. The house contains outstanding Robert Adam interiors, original Chinese wallpapers and an important collection of Joshua Reynolds paintings. The grounds include a beautiful orangery, formal gardens and 500 acres of parkland with views across the Plym estuary.
shortDescription: Grand Georgian mansion near Plymouth with Robert Adam interiors and sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Killerton
description: Killerton is an 18th-century house set in extensive hillside gardens and a sweeping Devon estate near Exeter. The house is known for its nationally important fashion collection, displayed in changing exhibitions throughout the year. The gardens are celebrated for their champion trees, rhododendrons, and herbaceous borders, while the wider estate includes farmland, orchards and woodland walks.
shortDescription: Devon hillside estate near Exeter with champion trees and a celebrated fashion collection.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castle Drogo
description: Castle Drogo is the last castle to be built in England, designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens and constructed between 1911 and 1930 high above the Teign Gorge on Dartmoor. The granite castle combines medieval grandeur with 20th-century comforts and has undergone a major conservation project. The garden features a formal parterre, circular croquet lawn and rose garden, with dramatic walks through the gorge below.
shortDescription: The last castle built in England, a Lutyens masterpiece above the Teign Gorge on Dartmoor.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Knightshayes
description: Knightshayes is a Victorian Gothic Revival house near Tiverton designed by William Burges, one of the most original architects of the 19th century. The richly decorated interiors include painted ceilings and elaborate carvings. The surrounding garden is one of the finest in Devon, with rare shrubs, a walled kitchen garden, a topiary fox hunt, and sweeping parkland views.
shortDescription: Gothic Revival house near Tiverton with Burges interiors and one of Devon's finest gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Greenway
description: Greenway is the beloved holiday home of the crime writer Agatha Christie, set in a romantic woodland garden on the banks of the River Dart. The house contains Christie's personal collections, books, archaeological finds and her drawing room where she entertained guests. The surrounding 30-acre garden descends to a boathouse on the river and includes rare champion trees.
shortDescription: Agatha Christie's riverside holiday home on the Dart with woodland gardens and personal collections.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Buckland Abbey
description: Buckland Abbey is a 700-year-old former Cistercian monastery that became the home of Sir Francis Drake in 1581. The abbey church was ingeniously converted into a house, and the property displays Drake memorabilia including his famous drum. Set in the Tavy Valley, the estate includes a medieval tithe barn, herb garden, and craft workshops.
shortDescription: Sir Francis Drake's monastery home in the Tavy Valley with his famous drum on display.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Arlington Court
description: Arlington Court is a Regency house set in a beautiful estate in North Devon, home to the Chichester family for over 500 years. The house contains an eclectic collection including model ships, shells and costume. The estate features a Victorian garden, lake, and carriage collection in the stables, as well as ancient woodland and a heronry.
shortDescription: Regency house in North Devon with eclectic collections, a carriage museum and Victorian gardens.

name: Coleton Fishacre
description: Coleton Fishacre is an Arts and Crafts-style house built in the 1920s for Rupert and Lady Dorothy D'Oyly Carte of Gilbert and Sullivan fame. The house overlooks a luxuriant garden that cascades through a valley to the sea at Pudcombe Cove. Rare and tender plants from around the world thrive in the sheltered microclimate, and the coastal paths offer superb walking.
shortDescription: 1920s Arts and Crafts house with a lush valley garden tumbling to the Devon coast.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Lacock Abbey
description: Lacock Abbey is a medieval nunnery converted into a country house after the dissolution, set in the picturesque village of Lacock. The house is celebrated as the birthplace of photography, where William Henry Fox Talbot created his first photographic negative in 1835. The abbey retains its medieval cloisters, and the museum tells the story of Talbot's pioneering experiments.
shortDescription: Medieval abbey and birthplace of photography, set in the picturesque village of Lacock.

name: Avebury
description: Avebury is the largest stone circle in Europe, enclosing the village of Avebury within its massive Neolithic bank and ditch. Built around 2850 BC, the monument is part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site that includes the West Kennet Avenue, Silbury Hill and the West Kennet Long Barrow. The Alexander Keiller Museum on site tells the story of the circle's rediscovery.
shortDescription: Europe's largest stone circle, a Neolithic UNESCO World Heritage Site enclosing a Wiltshire village.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Mompesson House
description: Mompesson House is an elegant Queen Anne townhouse in Salisbury's Cathedral Close, featured as the Dashwood London townhouse in the 1995 film Sense and Sensibility. The house contains a fine collection of 18th-century drinking glasses and period furnishings. A beautiful walled garden provides a tranquil retreat steps from the cathedral.
shortDescription: Elegant Queen Anne townhouse in Salisbury Cathedral Close with a fine glass collection and walled garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Dunham Massey
description: Dunham Massey is a Georgian house set in a magnificent deer park on the outskirts of Altrincham. The house contains outstanding collections of Huguenot silver, paintings and furniture amassed by the Booth and Grey families. The ancient deer park is home to one of England's largest herds of fallow deer, and the winter garden is celebrated for its bark, berries and scented flowers.
shortDescription: Georgian house in a deer park near Altrincham with Huguenot silver and a famous winter garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Quarry Bank
description: Quarry Bank is one of Britain's greatest industrial heritage sites, centred on a Georgian cotton mill in the wooded Bollin Valley at Styal. Founded by Samuel Greg in 1784, the mill retains working textile machinery powered by Europe's most powerful waterwheel. The Apprentice House reveals the harsh lives of child workers, while the surrounding estate includes gardens and riverside walks.
shortDescription: Georgian cotton mill at Styal with working machinery, Apprentice House and riverside walks.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Lyme
description: Lyme is a grand Elizabethan mansion set in a medieval deer park on the edge of the Peak District near Stockport. Home to the Legh family for over 600 years, the house contains Mortlake tapestries and an important collection of English clocks. The park's ancient herd of red deer, Edwardian rose garden, and moorland walks make it a major visitor attraction.
shortDescription: Elizabethan mansion in a medieval deer park on the edge of the Peak District.

name: Tatton Park
description: Tatton Park is a grand neo-classical mansion set in 1,000 acres of deer park near Knutsford. The opulent Egerton family home contains Gillow furniture, a picture gallery and servants' quarters. The gardens are among the most important in England, featuring a Japanese garden, Italian garden, glasshouses and the national collection of tree ferns.
shortDescription: Neo-classical mansion near Knutsford with opulent interiors and nationally important gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Little Moreton Hall
description: Little Moreton Hall is one of the finest examples of a timber-framed moated manor house in England, dating from the mid-15th century. The wonky black-and-white building, with its elaborate carved gables and long gallery, appears to defy gravity. A knot garden based on a 17th-century design and a small border garden surround the moat.
shortDescription: Iconic wonky timber-framed moated manor house, one of England's finest medieval buildings.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Alderley Edge and Cheshire Countryside
description: Alderley Edge is a dramatic sandstone escarpment rising above the Cheshire Plain, steeped in legend and mining history dating back to the Bronze Age. Woodland walks thread through beech, pine and oak, passing old copper mines and the Wizard's Well. From the Edge, views stretch across the plain to Manchester and the Peak District hills.
shortDescription: Legendary sandstone escarpment above the Cheshire Plain with ancient mines and woodland walks.

name: Castlefield Viaduct
description: Castlefield Viaduct is a Victorian railway viaduct in the heart of Manchester, reimagined as an elevated park and green space. Inspired by New York's High Line, the viaduct features planters with trees, grasses and wildflowers creating an urban oasis. It offers a unique elevated perspective over the historic Castlefield Basin and the city's canal network.
shortDescription: Victorian railway viaduct in central Manchester transformed into an elevated urban park.

name: Hare Hill
description: Hare Hill is an intimate woodland garden near Macclesfield, celebrated for its spectacular display of rhododendrons and azaleas in late spring. A walled garden at the heart of the property provides a sheltered haven with year-round interest. Surrounding parkland and woodland offer peaceful walks with views towards the Peak District.
shortDescription: Intimate woodland garden near Macclesfield famed for its spectacular rhododendron displays.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Additional Family e.g. 1 adult and 2 children

name: Nether Alderley Mill
description: Nether Alderley Mill is a rare 16th-century watermill hidden beneath the sandstone ridge of Alderley Edge. The mill, powered by an overshot waterwheel, still grinds flour on demonstration days using its original stone mechanisms. The atmospheric interior and woodland setting provide a glimpse into centuries of rural Cheshire life.
shortDescription: Rare 16th-century watermill beneath Alderley Edge, still grinding flour on demonstration days.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Speke Hall
description: Speke Hall is one of England's finest surviving Tudor timber-framed houses, set in tranquil gardens and woodland on the banks of the Mersey near Liverpool Airport. Built over a century from 1530, the house features priest holes, elaborate plasterwork, and a great hall. The restored Victorian gardens include the Adam and Eve topiaries and a kitchen garden.
shortDescription: Magnificent Tudor timber-framed house on the Mersey with priest holes and Victorian gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: The Beatles' Childhood Homes
description: The Beatles' Childhood Homes are two houses in Liverpool where John Lennon and Paul McCartney grew up. Mendips, John's childhood home with Aunt Mimi, is a 1930s semi-detached where he wrote early songs. 20 Forthlin Road, Paul's terraced council house, is where many of the earliest Lennon-McCartney songs were composed. Both can only be visited on guided minibus tours from the city centre.
shortDescription: John Lennon's and Paul McCartney's childhood homes, accessible by guided minibus tour only.

name: Rufford Old Hall
description: Rufford Old Hall is one of Lancashire's finest Tudor buildings, famous for its extravagant Great Hall with an intricately carved movable wooden screen. A young William Shakespeare is said to have performed here in the 1580s. The Victorian wing houses the Hesketh family's eclectic collections, and the gardens include a topiary avenue and seasonal herbaceous borders.
shortDescription: Fine Tudor hall in Lancashire where Shakespeare may have performed, with a famous carved screen.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Gawthorpe Hall
description: Gawthorpe Hall is an Elizabethan gem set on the banks of the River Calder near Burnley, restored in the 1850s by Sir Charles Barry of Houses of Parliament fame. The house contains the Rachel Kay-Shuttleworth textile collection, one of the most important in Europe. The grounds include a formal parterre garden and woodland walks along the river.
shortDescription: Elizabethan hall near Burnley with Barry interiors and a world-class textile collection.

name: Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal
description: Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site near Ripon, combining the spectacular ruins of a 12th-century Cistercian monastery with one of the finest surviving Georgian water gardens in England. The estate also includes a medieval deer park, St Mary's Church designed by William Burges, and an Elizabethan mansion. It is one of the National Trust's most visited properties.
shortDescription: UNESCO World Heritage Site combining Cistercian abbey ruins with a Georgian water garden near Ripon.

name: Nunnington Hall
description: Nunnington Hall is a beautiful manor house in the Ryedale valley of the North York Moors, with parts dating from the Tudor and Stuart periods. The house contains panelled rooms, tapestries, and the Carlisle Collection of miniature rooms displayed in the attic. Organic gardens lead down to the River Rye, and the surrounding countryside offers gentle walks.
shortDescription: Tudor and Stuart manor in Ryedale with miniature rooms, organic gardens and river walks.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Beningbrough Hall
description: Beningbrough Hall is an imposing Georgian baroque mansion near York, built in 1716 with stunning wood carvings and a dramatic two-storey great hall. The house displays over 100 portraits from the National Portrait Gallery collection. The surrounding gardens and parkland include a walled garden, an orchard, and a wilderness play area for children.
shortDescription: Georgian baroque mansion near York displaying National Portrait Gallery works in grand interiors.

name: Brimham Rocks
description: Brimham Rocks is a collection of extraordinary weathered gritstone formations scattered across Nidderdale moorland above the Nidd Valley. Sculpted over 320 million years by ice, wind and water, the rocks form dramatic towers, arches and balanced boulders with names like the Idol, the Sphinx and the Dancing Bear. The site offers family-friendly scrambling and sweeping views of the Yorkshire Dales.
shortDescription: Extraordinary weathered gritstone formations on Nidderdale moorland with panoramic Yorkshire views.

name: Rievaulx Terrace
description: Rievaulx Terrace is an 18th-century landscaped terrace above the ruins of Rievaulx Abbey in the Rye Valley. The half-mile grass terrace was designed to provide a series of carefully framed views down to the medieval abbey below. Two classical temples punctuate the walk, one containing a painted ceiling and period furnishings, offering a perfect marriage of art and landscape.
shortDescription: Georgian landscaped terrace with classical temples and framed views of Rievaulx Abbey ruins.

name: Nostell Priory
description: Nostell Priory is a Palladian mansion near Wakefield, built on the site of a medieval priory and completed by Robert Adam in the 1760s. The house contains one of the finest collections of Chippendale furniture in the world, made specifically for the property. The parkland, landscaped by Stephen Switzer, includes a lake, an adventure playground and lakeside walks.
shortDescription: Palladian mansion near Wakefield with the finest collection of Chippendale furniture in the world.

name: East Riddlesden Hall
description: East Riddlesden Hall is a 17th-century manor house set in grounds beside the River Aire in Keighley. The dark stone building features a stunning medieval tithe barn, one of the finest in the north of England. Inside, the hall contains period furnishings and displays that bring to life the stories of its former inhabitants.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century manor house with a magnificent medieval tithe barn in Keighley.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Clumber Park
description: Clumber Park is a vast expanse of parkland, heath and woods covering 3,800 acres in the heart of Sherwood Forest. Once the country estate of the Dukes of Newcastle, the park features a magnificent double lime tree avenue, a serpentine lake, and a striking Gothic Revival chapel. It is one of Nottinghamshire's most popular green spaces for walking and cycling.
shortDescription: A sweeping 3,800-acre parkland in Sherwood Forest with a grand Gothic chapel and serpentine lake.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Mr Straw's House
description: Mr Straw's House is an Edwardian semi-detached house in Worksop preserved almost exactly as the Straw family left it. The brothers William and Walter Straw changed very little after their parents' deaths, creating a time capsule of early 20th-century domestic life. Visits are by timed ticket only, offering an intimate glimpse into a remarkably ordinary yet fascinating household.
shortDescription: An Edwardian time-capsule house in Worksop, preserved untouched since the early 20th century.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Shugborough Estate
description: Shugborough Estate is the ancestral seat of the Earls of Lichfield, set in 900 acres of Staffordshire parkland. The mansion house contains fine collections of ceramics, silver and paintings, while the surrounding grounds feature neo-classical monuments and a working rare breeds farm. The estate also includes a restored Georgian servants' quarters and a walled garden.
shortDescription: A grand 900-acre Staffordshire estate with a mansion, neo-classical monuments, and working farm.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Biddulph Grange Garden
description: Biddulph Grange Garden is a remarkable Victorian garden that takes visitors on a tour of the world through themed areas including an Egyptian court, a Chinese garden and an Italian terrace. Created by James Bateman in the mid-19th century, it is one of the most unusual and creative gardens in England. The garden also features a stunning dahlia walk and geological gallery.
shortDescription: A theatrical Victorian garden with themed areas transporting visitors from China to Egypt.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wightwick Manor
description: Wightwick Manor is a Victorian manor house built in the Arts and Crafts style, filled with an outstanding collection of Pre-Raphaelite art including works by Rossetti, Burne-Jones and William Morris. The house is complemented by beautiful gardens with topiary, terraces and a woodland garden. It offers one of the finest collections of Pre-Raphaelite paintings outside a public gallery.
shortDescription: A Victorian Arts and Crafts manor filled with Pre-Raphaelite masterpieces near Wolverhampton.
