name: Birmingham Back to Backs
description: Birmingham Back to Backs is the last surviving court of back-to-back houses in the city, telling the stories of the people who lived there from the 1840s to the 1970s. Guided tours reveal rooms furnished and decorated to represent different eras, from a Victorian tailor's workshop to a 1970s sweetshop. The houses provide a vivid and moving picture of working-class life in Birmingham.
shortDescription: The last surviving court of Birmingham's back-to-back houses, spanning 130 years of working-class life.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cragside
description: Cragside was the home of Victorian inventor and industrialist Lord Armstrong and was the first house in the world to be lit by hydroelectric power. The dramatic house is perched on a rocky crag above the Debdon Burn, surrounded by one of the largest rock gardens in Europe and over 1,700 acres of forest and moorland. The estate contains over 7 million trees and 40 miles of footpaths.
shortDescription: Lord Armstrong's revolutionary Victorian mansion, the first house lit by hydroelectric power.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Wallington
description: Wallington is a grand yet welcoming country house set in beautiful grounds in rural Northumberland. The interior features Pre-Raphaelite paintings, fine porcelain and an impressive central hall decorated with murals depicting the history of the region. The walled garden, woodland walks and adventure playground make it a rewarding place for families.
shortDescription: A welcoming Northumberland country house with Pre-Raphaelite murals and beautiful walled gardens.

name: Lindisfarne Castle
description: Lindisfarne Castle stands on a dramatic rocky promontory on Holy Island, accessible only by a tidal causeway from the Northumberland coast. Originally a Tudor fort, it was converted into a private home by architect Edwin Lutyens in 1903. The castle features a small but charming walled garden designed by Gertrude Jekyll and offers spectacular views across the sea and coastline.
shortDescription: A dramatic Tudor-fort-turned-Lutyens-home on Holy Island, reached by tidal causeway.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Seaton Delaval Hall
description: Seaton Delaval Hall is considered one of Sir John Vanbrugh's finest architectural works, a theatrical English Baroque masterpiece on the Northumberland coast. The central block was gutted by fire in 1822 and stands as a dramatic shell, while the flanking wings have been carefully restored. The gardens include a parterre, wildflower meadows and a recently restored play garden.
shortDescription: Vanbrugh's theatrical Baroque masterpiece on the Northumberland coast, dramatically fire-scarred and restored.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Gibside
description: Gibside is a Georgian landscape garden and forest estate in the Derwent Valley near Gateshead. Once the ancestral home of the Bowes-Lyon family, the estate features the stunning Palladian Chapel, a grand Column of Liberty and miles of riverside and woodland walks. The ruined Gibside Hall stands as a reminder of the estate's former grandeur.
shortDescription: A Georgian forest estate in the Derwent Valley with a Palladian chapel and Bowes-Lyon heritage.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Souter Lighthouse
description: Souter Lighthouse was the first lighthouse in the world to be purpose-built for electric light when it opened in 1871. Perched on the clifftops at Marsden between the Tyne and the Wear, it offers panoramic views along the dramatic coastline. The surrounding grasslands are home to wildflowers and nesting seabirds, and the Leas below provide excellent rock-pool exploring.
shortDescription: The world's first purpose-built electric lighthouse, set on dramatic Marsden clifftops.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Washington Old Hall
description: Washington Old Hall is a modest stone manor house that was home to the ancestors of George Washington, the first President of the United States. The present building dates mainly from the 17th century and contains period furnishings along with displays charting the Washington family's history. A small formal garden complements the house.
shortDescription: The ancestral home of George Washington's family, a 17th-century stone manor in Tyne and Wear.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Hill Top
description: Hill Top is the 17th-century farmhouse where Beatrix Potter wrote and illustrated many of her beloved children's stories. The house is preserved exactly as she left it, filled with her furniture, china and personal belongings. The cottage garden provided inspiration for many of her illustrations and remains a highlight of any visit to the Lake District.
shortDescription: Beatrix Potter's beloved 17th-century farmhouse, preserved as she left it in Near Sawrey.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Sizergh
description: Sizergh has been home to the Strickland family for over 760 years, making it one of the longest-held family homes in England. The medieval castle features a remarkable Elizabethan carved overmantel and impressive panelling. The surrounding estate includes a limestone rock garden, wildflower meadows, an orchard and a lake with stunning views towards the Lakeland fells.
shortDescription: A castle held by the Strickland family for 760 years, with Elizabethan interiors and a rock garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wordsworth House
description: Wordsworth House is the Georgian townhouse in Cockermouth where the poet William Wordsworth was born in 1770 and spent his formative childhood years. The house has been furnished to reflect life in the 1770s, with hands-on displays and costumed guides bringing the period to life. The riverside garden, which Wordsworth described in The Prelude, runs down to the River Derwent.
shortDescription: The Georgian birthplace of William Wordsworth in Cockermouth, furnished as it was in the 1770s.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wray Castle
description: Wray Castle is a Gothic Revival castle on the western shore of Windermere, built in the 1840s for a retired surgeon. The young Beatrix Potter spent a holiday here in 1882, sparking her lifelong love of the Lake District. The castle interior is largely unfurnished but hosts interactive displays and dressing-up activities for families.
shortDescription: A Gothic Revival castle on Windermere's shore where Beatrix Potter first fell in love with the Lakes.

name: Acorn Bank
description: Acorn Bank is home to the largest collection of medicinal and culinary herbs in the north of England, set within the sheltered walled garden of a former manor house. The surrounding orchards contain over 100 varieties of traditional apple and other fruit trees. Woodland walks lead down to the dramatic Crowdundle Beck waterfall and the remains of a former corn mill.
shortDescription: A walled herb garden with the north's largest medicinal plant collection, near a hidden waterfall.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Fell Foot
description: Fell Foot is a beautiful Victorian park at the southern tip of Windermere, offering direct access to the lake shore. The restored boathouses provide rowing boat and kayak hire, while the sweeping lawns and mature trees make it a perfect spot for picnics. The park also features a restored Victorian garden, an adventure playground and a cafe with lake views.
shortDescription: A Victorian lakeside park at the southern tip of Windermere with boat hire and sweeping lawns.

name: Aira Force and Gowbarrow Park
description: Aira Force is a spectacular 20-metre waterfall set in ancient woodland on the shores of Ullswater. The surrounding Gowbarrow Park is where Wordsworth and his sister Dorothy famously saw the daffodils that inspired one of the best-known poems in the English language. Stone-arched footbridges span the gorge above the waterfall, offering dramatic viewpoints.
shortDescription: A stunning 20-metre waterfall in ancient woodland, where Wordsworth found his famous daffodils.

name: Allan Bank and Grasmere
description: Allan Bank is an informal country house above Grasmere that was once home to both William Wordsworth and John Ruskin. Partly damaged by fire in 2011, the house has been left deliberately unrestored to create a relaxed, welcoming space with stunning views over Grasmere lake and vale. Visitors are encouraged to explore freely, play games and enjoy the grounds.
shortDescription: An informal Lakeland house above Grasmere, once home to Wordsworth and Ruskin.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Arnside and Silverdale
description: Arnside and Silverdale is an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty on the shores of Morecambe Bay, where limestone pavements, ancient woodland and salt marshes meet the coast. The area is renowned for its rich wildlife including butterflies, wildflowers and wading birds. The Trust cares for Eaves Wood and Jack Scout, both offering walks with spectacular views across the Bay.
shortDescription: A limestone coast on Morecambe Bay with rich wildlife, ancient woods and bay views.

name: Borrowdale and Derwent Water
description: Borrowdale is one of the most beautiful valleys in the Lake District, stretching from the shores of Derwent Water southward into dramatic mountain scenery. The Trust owns much of the valley including ancient oak woodland at Johnny Wood and the famous Bowder Stone, a 2,000-tonne boulder balanced on one edge. The area offers walks of all levels from gentle lakeside strolls to high fell routes.
shortDescription: One of the Lake District's most beautiful valleys, with ancient woodland and dramatic mountain scenery.

name: Buttermere Valley
description: Buttermere Valley contains two of the Lake District's most photogenic lakes, Buttermere and Crummock Water, set beneath dramatic fell scenery. The easy lakeside walk around Buttermere is one of the most popular in the region, passing through a rock tunnel and offering views of Haystacks and Fleetwith Pike. The valley is also the starting point for ascents of several classic Lakeland peaks.
shortDescription: Twin lakes beneath dramatic fells, with one of the Lake District's finest easy lakeside walks.

name: Claife Viewing Station and Windermere West Shore
description: Claife Viewing Station is a restored Gothic ruin on the western shore of Windermere, originally built in 1799 for tourists seeking picturesque views. Coloured glass windows once framed the lake in different seasonal moods. The surrounding woodland paths lead through ancient coppiced woods down to the quiet western shore of England's longest lake.
shortDescription: A restored Gothic viewing station on Windermere's tranquil western shore with woodland walks.

name: Dalton Castle
description: Dalton Castle is a 14th-century pele tower in the centre of Dalton-in-Furness, once the administrative seat of the Abbot of Furness Abbey. The three-storey tower was used as a courthouse and prison and now houses a small local history museum. It stands in the town's main square and is open on a limited seasonal basis.
shortDescription: A 14th-century pele tower in Dalton-in-Furness, once the courthouse for Furness Abbey.

name: Ennerdale
description: Ennerdale is one of the quietest and most unspoilt valleys in the Lake District, with no road running along the lake. The valley is at the heart of the Wild Ennerdale project, which is allowing the landscape to develop naturally with minimal human intervention. Ancient forest, open fell and the remote lake create a true sense of wilderness.
shortDescription: The Lake District's wildest valley, with no road and a pioneering rewilding project.

name: Eskdale and Duddon Valley
description: Eskdale and the Duddon Valley are neighbouring valleys in the western Lake District, both celebrated for their unspoilt beauty and relative quietness. Eskdale is home to the narrow-gauge Ravenglass and Eskdale Railway and the remains of a Roman bathhouse at Hardknott Fort. The Duddon Valley was a favourite of Wordsworth, who wrote a sequence of sonnets about the River Duddon.
shortDescription: Quiet western valleys with a Roman fort, miniature railway and Wordsworth's beloved River Duddon.

name: Historic Steam Yacht Gondola
description: The Steam Yacht Gondola is a beautifully restored Victorian steam-powered vessel that has been sailing on Coniston Water since 1859. Rebuilt by the National Trust in the 1980s, she carries passengers on scenic cruises past Brantwood (John Ruskin's home) and through stunning mountain scenery. The opulently upholstered saloons evoke the elegance of Victorian lake travel.
shortDescription: A restored Victorian steam yacht offering scenic cruises on Coniston Water since 1859.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Keld Chapel
description: Keld Chapel is a tiny pre-Reformation chapel tucked away in the hamlet of Keld near Shap. Dating from the early 16th century, it is one of very few surviving buildings of its kind in the region. The simple stone building has been sensitively maintained and is freely accessible to visitors year-round.
shortDescription: A rare pre-Reformation chapel from the early 1500s, tucked away in a quiet hamlet near Shap.

name: Sandscale Haws National Nature Reserve
description: Sandscale Haws is a National Nature Reserve on the Duddon Estuary, comprising a large dune system rich in wildlife. The reserve supports rare natterjack toads, orchids and over 30 species of butterfly. Boardwalks and paths wind through the dunes, offering views across the estuary to the Lakeland fells and out to the Irish Sea.
shortDescription: A wildlife-rich dune reserve on the Duddon Estuary, home to rare natterjack toads and orchids.

name: Stagshaw Garden and Ambleside
description: Stagshaw Garden is an informal woodland garden on the hillside above Ambleside, created by the former regional National Trust agent Cubby Acland. The garden is celebrated for its magnificent collection of azaleas, rhododendrons and camellias, which create a spectacular display in spring. Paths wind through the garden offering glimpses of Windermere through the trees.
shortDescription: A hillside woodland garden above Ambleside famed for its spring azalea and rhododendron displays.

name: Tarn Hows and Coniston
description: Tarn Hows is one of the most visited and photographed spots in the Lake District, a picturesque man-made tarn surrounded by woodland and hills with views to the Langdale Pikes and the Old Man of Coniston. An easy circular path of about a mile and a half loops around the tarn, making it accessible for families and those with limited mobility. The site was donated to the Trust by Beatrix Potter.
shortDescription: One of the Lake District's most iconic viewpoints, a picturesque tarn donated by Beatrix Potter.

name: The Langdales
description: The Langdales encompass two of the Lake District's most dramatic valleys, Great Langdale and Little Langdale, surrounded by some of the finest peaks in England. The distinctive profile of the Langdale Pikes is one of the most recognisable mountain silhouettes in Britain. The valleys contain a Neolithic stone axe factory site and offer walks ranging from gentle riverside paths to challenging fell scrambles.
shortDescription: Twin Lakeland valleys beneath the iconic Langdale Pikes, with walks from gentle to challenging.

name: Townend
description: Townend is a well-preserved 17th-century Lakeland farmhouse in the village of Troutbeck, home to the Browne family for over 300 years. The house contains an exceptional collection of carved woodwork, books, papers and domestic implements that record the life of a prosperous yeoman farming family. It is one of the finest examples of Lake District vernacular architecture.
shortDescription: A 17th-century Troutbeck farmhouse with exceptional carved woodwork, home to one family for 300 years.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Ullswater
description: Ullswater is the second largest lake in the Lake District, stretching seven and a half miles through three dramatic reaches. The National Trust cares for much of the surrounding land including the famous Glenridding Common, Glencoyne and the slopes of Helvellyn. The shores provide the setting for Wordsworth's daffodils and offer some of the finest scenery in England.
shortDescription: The Lake District's second largest lake with dramatic mountain scenery and Wordsworth's daffodil shores.

name: Wasdale
description: Wasdale is one of the most dramatic valleys in the Lake District, home to England's deepest lake (Wast Water) and highest mountain (Scafell Pike). The iconic view of the Wasdale Head screes plunging into the dark waters of the lake is one of the most photographed scenes in the national park. The valley is a key starting point for ascents of Scafell Pike and Great Gable.
shortDescription: England's deepest lake and highest mountain meet in this dramatic and iconic Lakeland valley.

name: Whitehaven Coast
description: The Whitehaven Coast stretches along the western edge of Cumbria, offering dramatic clifftop walks with views across the Irish Sea to the Isle of Man and Scotland. The Trust cares for stretches of this coastline including the restored Haig Colliery mining museum site at Whitehaven. Red sandstone cliffs, shingle beaches and industrial heritage combine along this underrated coastline.
shortDescription: Dramatic red sandstone cliffs on Cumbria's west coast with views to the Isle of Man.

name: Powis Castle and Garden
description: Powis Castle is a medieval fortress that has been continuously inhabited for over 700 years, rising above world-famous Italianate terraced gardens. The castle houses one of the finest collections of paintings, sculpture, furniture and textiles in Wales, alongside the Clive Museum of treasures from India. The terraces, originally laid out in the late 17th century, are among the finest in Britain.
shortDescription: A 700-year-old medieval castle above world-famous Italianate terraced gardens in Powys.

name: Bodnant Garden
description: Bodnant Garden is one of the finest gardens in the United Kingdom, set in 80 acres above the River Conwy with views to Snowdonia. Created over 150 years by four generations of the Aberconway family, it features sweeping Italianate terraces, a dell garden with rare trees and the famous Laburnum Arch. The garden is spectacular in every season, from spring azaleas to autumn colour.
shortDescription: One of Britain's finest gardens, famed for its Laburnum Arch and Snowdonia views.

name: Penrhyn Castle
description: Penrhyn Castle is a monumental neo-Norman castle near Bangor, built in the early 19th century for the Pennant family with wealth from Jamaican sugar plantations and Welsh slate quarries. The extravagant interior includes a one-tonne slate bed made for Queen Victoria and an outstanding collection of Old Master paintings. The castle also tells the story of the Great Strike of 1900-03 at the Penrhyn Quarry.
shortDescription: A colossal neo-Norman castle near Bangor built on slate wealth, with a one-tonne slate bed.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Plas Newydd
description: Plas Newydd is an elegant 18th-century mansion on the shores of the Menai Strait on Anglesey, home to the Marquesses of Anglesey. The house contains a spectacular 58-foot mural by Rex Whistler, one of the largest paintings on canvas in Britain. The gardens and grounds offer stunning views across the strait to the mountains of Snowdonia, and include a rhododendron garden and Australasian arboretum.
shortDescription: An elegant Anglesey mansion with Rex Whistler's largest mural and Menai Strait views to Snowdonia.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Erddig
description: Erddig is a remarkable country house near Wrexham that tells the story of the relationship between a wealthy family and their servants over 250 years. The house contains the most detailed collection of servants' portraits and histories of any country house in Britain. The estate includes a restored 18th-century formal garden, a walled garden and 1,200 acres of parkland.
shortDescription: A Wrexham country house uniquely telling the story of both the family and their servants over 250 years.

name: Chirk Castle
description: Chirk Castle is a magnificent medieval fortress completed in 1310, the last of Edward I's chain of castles and the only one that has been continuously inhabited for over 700 years. The interior spans from medieval dungeon to elegant state rooms with fine furniture and tapestries. The award-winning gardens feature clipped yew hedges, herbaceous borders and a stunning wrought-iron gate made by the Davies brothers in 1719.
shortDescription: The last of Edward I's Welsh castles, continuously lived in for over 700 years.

name: Plas yn Rhiw
description: Plas yn Rhiw is a small manor house with an ornamental garden and stunning views over Cardigan Bay on the Llyn Peninsula. The house dates partly from the medieval period and was rescued from neglect by the Keating sisters in 1938. Its sheltered gardens feature box hedges, grass paths, and flowering trees set against a backdrop of Snowdonia.
shortDescription: A rescued medieval manor house with ornamental gardens overlooking Cardigan Bay on the Llyn Peninsula.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Dinefwr
description: Dinefwr is a historic estate in the Tywi Valley comprising Newton House, a medieval castle, and a stunning deer park designed by Capability Brown. The castle perched on a rocky hilltop was once the seat of the Welsh princes of Deheubarth. The parkland is home to a herd of White Park cattle, one of the rarest breeds in the world.
shortDescription: A Capability Brown deer park estate with Newton House and a medieval Welsh castle.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Llanerchaeron
description: Llanerchaeron is a self-sufficient 18th-century Welsh gentry estate designed by John Nash, set in the Aeron valley. The property includes a villa, walled gardens, a home farm with original outbuildings, and a lake. It offers a rare surviving example of the type of estate that was once common across Wales.
shortDescription: A John Nash-designed 18th-century Welsh gentry estate with walled gardens and a working home farm.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Tredegar House
description: Tredegar House is one of the finest late 17th-century mansions in Wales, set within 90 acres of beautiful parkland on the outskirts of Newport. Home to the Morgan family for over 500 years, the house features lavish state rooms and restored service areas. The grounds include formal gardens, an orangery, and a lakeside walk.
shortDescription: One of Wales's finest 17th-century mansions with lavish interiors set within 90 acres of parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 Children)

name: Dyffryn Gardens
description: Dyffryn Gardens is a stunning Edwardian garden spread over 55 acres in the Vale of Glamorgan, designed by landscape architect Thomas Mawson. The gardens feature a series of themed garden rooms, an arboretum with champion trees, and a grand lawn. The restored glasshouse contains tropical and cacti collections.
shortDescription: A 55-acre Edwardian garden with themed garden rooms designed by Thomas Mawson in the Vale of Glamorgan.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Aberdulais Falls
description: Aberdulais Falls is a picturesque waterfall on the River Dulais that has powered industry for over 400 years, from copper smelting to tin plating. The site now houses a hydroelectric scheme generating green energy and a fish pass for migrating sea trout. Visitors can explore the historic waterwheel, turbine, and waterfall viewpoints.
shortDescription: A historic waterfall powering industry for four centuries, now home to a hydroelectric scheme and fish pass.

name: Dolaucothi Gold Mines
description: Dolaucothi Gold Mines is the only known Roman gold mine in Britain, set in the wooded Cothi Valley in Carmarthenshire. Visitors can take guided underground tours through tunnels dating back nearly 2,000 years. The site also offers surface trails and gold panning experiences that reveal the area's rich mining heritage.
shortDescription: The only known Roman gold mine in Britain, offering guided underground tours and gold panning.

name: Colby Woodland Garden
description: Colby Woodland Garden is a tranquil eight-acre garden set in a secluded valley near Amroth in Pembrokeshire. The garden is known for its spectacular spring displays of rhododendrons, azaleas, and bluebells, along with a walled garden restored to its former glory. A meadow and woodland walk lead down to a stream and peaceful pond.
shortDescription: A tranquil woodland garden in Pembrokeshire known for spectacular spring rhododendron and bluebell displays.

name: Stackpole
description: Stackpole is a spectacular coastal estate in south Pembrokeshire encompassing limestone cliffs, lily ponds, woodlands, and the famous Barafundle Bay beach. The estate's eight-mile trail network passes through diverse habitats home to otters, choughs, and rare orchids. Bosherston Lily Ponds are particularly stunning when the water lilies bloom in June.
shortDescription: A stunning Pembrokeshire coastal estate with Barafundle Bay, lily ponds, and limestone cliff walks.

name: Tudor Merchant's House
description: Tudor Merchant's House is a late 15th-century townhouse on Quay Hill in Tenby, built for a prosperous merchant when the town was a thriving trading port. The three-storey building retains its original chimney and traces of decorative wall paintings. It offers a vivid glimpse of domestic and commercial life in Tudor Wales.
shortDescription: A late 15th-century merchant's townhouse in Tenby with original features and Tudor wall paintings.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Giant's Causeway
description: Giant's Causeway is a UNESCO World Heritage Site on the north Antrim coast, famous for its 40,000 interlocking basalt columns formed by volcanic activity 60 million years ago. Legend attributes the columns to the giant Finn McCool who built a causeway to Scotland. The visitor centre tells the geological and mythological story of this extraordinary landscape.
shortDescription: A UNESCO World Heritage Site of 40,000 volcanic basalt columns steeped in the legend of Finn McCool.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge
description: Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge is a famous rope bridge near Ballintoy that spans a 20-metre chasm between the mainland and a small island. Originally erected by salmon fishermen over 350 years ago, the bridge offers breathtaking views of Rathlin Island and the Scottish coast. The surrounding coastal path features dramatic cliff scenery and seabird colonies.
shortDescription: A thrilling rope bridge spanning a coastal chasm with views to Rathlin Island and Scotland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: The Crown Bar
description: The Crown Bar is a magnificently preserved Victorian gin palace on Great Victoria Street in Belfast, and the most famous pub in Northern Ireland. Its ornate interior features carved woodwork, coloured glass, decorative tiles, and original gas lighting. The National Trust acquired the building in 1978 to protect this outstanding example of Victorian craftsmanship.
shortDescription: Belfast's most famous Victorian gin palace with ornate carved woodwork and original gas lighting.

name: Divis and Black Mountain
description: Divis and Black Mountain rise above Belfast providing panoramic views across the city, Belfast Lough, and on clear days to Scotland, the Isle of Man, and the Mourne Mountains. The upland heath and bog landscape is an important habitat for birds including meadow pipits, skylarks, and peregrine falcons. Waymarked trails make this a popular walking destination for locals and visitors.
shortDescription: Upland heath above Belfast offering panoramic views across six counties on a clear day.

name: Dunseverick Castle
description: Dunseverick Castle stands as a dramatic ruin on a rocky headland overlooking the Atlantic on the north Antrim coast between the Giant's Causeway and Ballintoy. Once one of the oldest and most strongly fortified places in Ireland, its origins date back over 1,500 years. Only a single wall of the gatehouse remains, perched above the crashing waves.
shortDescription: Dramatic ruins of an ancient fortification on a rocky headland on the north Antrim coast.

name: Fair Head
description: Fair Head is a dramatic headland near Ballycastle with towering dolerite cliffs rising 196 metres above the sea, making it the highest cliff face in Northern Ireland. The summit offers stunning views across to Rathlin Island, the Mull of Kintyre, and the Scottish islands. Three loughs sit at the clifftop and the area is popular with rock climbers and walkers.
shortDescription: Northern Ireland's highest cliff face with views to Rathlin Island and Scotland.

name: Patterson's Spade Mill
description: Patterson's Spade Mill near Templepatrick is the last surviving water-driven spade mill in daily use in the British Isles. Founded in 1919, the mill demonstrates the complete spade-making process from hammer to finish using water-powered trip hammers. Guided tours show visitors the traditional techniques that once served farming communities across Ireland.
shortDescription: The last working water-driven spade mill in the British Isles, demonstrating traditional craftsmanship.

name: White Park Bay
description: White Park Bay is a stunning crescent of white sand stretching between two headlands on the north Antrim coast. The bay has Neolithic and Bronze Age archaeological significance, with flint tools and pottery found in its sand dunes. Chalk grassland above the beach supports rare plants and butterflies, and the bay is overlooked by dramatic limestone cliffs.
shortDescription: A sweeping crescent of white sand beach on the north Antrim coast with Neolithic archaeological remains.

name: Cushendun
description: Cushendun is a picturesque coastal village at the foot of Glendun on the Antrim coast, known for its distinctive Cornish-style cottages designed by Clough Williams-Ellis. The National Trust cares for the village and the surrounding caves, cliffs, and river estuary. The Cushendun Caves, carved from red sandstone, gained fame as a Game of Thrones filming location.
shortDescription: A picturesque Antrim coast village with Cornish-style cottages and red sandstone caves.

name: Islandmagee
description: Islandmagee is a peninsula on the east Antrim coast between Larne Lough and the Irish Sea, featuring dramatic basalt cliffs known as the Gobbins. The National Trust protects stretches of this rugged coastline, which shelters rock pools, seabird colonies, and wildflower-rich grassland. Brown's Bay offers safe swimming and views across to the Scottish coast.
shortDescription: A rugged east Antrim peninsula with dramatic basalt cliffs, rock pools, and coastal grassland.

name: Templetown Mausoleum
description: Templetown Mausoleum is an 18th-century mausoleum designed by Robert Adam, standing in the grounds of Castle Upton near Templepatrick. Built around 1789 as a memorial to the Hon. Arthur Upton, it is considered one of Adam's finest works in Ireland. The neoclassical structure features elegant proportions and decorative urns.
shortDescription: An elegant 18th-century neoclassical mausoleum designed by Robert Adam near Templepatrick.

name: Mount Stewart
description: Mount Stewart is a magnificent 18th-century house and garden on the shores of Strangford Lough in County Down. The gardens, created by Edith, Lady Londonderry in the 1920s, are among the finest in the National Trust's care, featuring exotic planting thriving in the mild microclimate. The house contains an outstanding collection of art and furnishings accumulated by the Londonderry family.
shortDescription: A magnificent 18th-century house on Strangford Lough with world-renowned gardens by Lady Londonderry.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castle Ward
description: Castle Ward is an 18th-century mansion in a stunning location overlooking Strangford Lough, famous for its two contrasting architectural facades: classical on the front and Gothic on the garden side. The 820-acre estate includes farmland, woodland, and lakeshore walks. The property is also well known as a Game of Thrones filming location for Winterfell.
shortDescription: An 18th-century mansion with two contrasting facades overlooking Strangford Lough, a Game of Thrones filming location.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Murlough National Nature Reserve
description: Murlough National Nature Reserve is one of Ireland's first nature reserves, protecting a fragile sand dune system at the foot of the Mourne Mountains. The dunes are among the oldest in Ireland, dating back 6,000 years, and support rare plants, butterflies, and birds. A boardwalk trail leads through the dunes to a sandy beach with views of the Mournes.
shortDescription: One of Ireland's oldest nature reserves with ancient sand dunes at the foot of the Mourne Mountains.

name: Rowallane Garden
description: Rowallane Garden is a 52-acre garden near Saintfield in County Down, renowned for its spectacular collection of rhododendrons, azaleas, and rare bulbs set within a walled garden and natural rock garden. Created over generations by the Rev. John Moore and his nephew Hugh Armytage Moore, the garden has an informal style that complements its drumlin landscape. Spring and autumn are particularly stunning.
shortDescription: A 52-acre garden near Saintfield renowned for rare rhododendrons and a natural rock garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Strangford Lough
description: Strangford Lough is a large sea lough in County Down and one of the most important wildlife habitats in Europe, designated as a Marine Nature Reserve. The lough is home to vast populations of wading birds and wintering wildfowl, as well as seals and rare marine life. The National Trust manages several key access points around the lough's shores and islands.
shortDescription: A vast sea lough in County Down that is one of Europe's most important marine wildlife habitats.

name: Castle Coole
description: Castle Coole is one of Ireland's finest neoclassical houses, designed by James Wyatt and completed in 1798, set in a landscaped parkland near Enniskillen. The interiors feature outstanding Regency furnishings including original silk curtains and a stunning state bedroom prepared for King George IV. The parkland is home to the oldest breeding colony of greylag geese in the British Isles.
shortDescription: One of Ireland's finest neoclassical houses by James Wyatt with Regency interiors near Enniskillen.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Florence Court
description: Florence Court is an elegant 18th-century house set against the backdrop of Cuilcagh Mountain in County Fermanagh. The house, home to the Cole family (Earls of Enniskillen), features fine rococo plasterwork and period furnishings. The grounds include a walled garden, pleasure grounds, and the parent tree of all Irish yew trees, propagated worldwide since 1767.
shortDescription: An elegant 18th-century Fermanagh mansion with fine rococo plasterwork and the original Irish yew tree.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Crom Estate
description: Crom Estate is one of Northern Ireland's most important nature conservation areas, comprising 1,900 acres of woodland, parkland, and wetland on the shores of Upper Lough Erne. The estate is home to rare species including pine martens, red squirrels, and one of Ireland's largest herds of fallow deer. The ruins of the old castle and two ancient yew trees add historical interest.
shortDescription: A 1,900-acre nature conservation estate on Upper Lough Erne with rare wildlife and castle ruins.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Downhill Demesne and Mussenden Temple
description: Downhill Demesne is a dramatic clifftop estate on the north coast of County Londonderry, best known for the iconic Mussenden Temple perched on the cliff edge above the Atlantic. Built in 1785 by Frederick Hervey, Earl-Bishop of Derry, as a library, the circular temple was modelled on the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli. The ruins of Downhill House and the walled garden are also within the demesne.
shortDescription: A dramatic clifftop estate with the iconic Mussenden Temple perched above the Atlantic Ocean.

name: Hezlett House
description: Hezlett House is a thatched 17th-century cottage near Castlerock in County Londonderry, one of the oldest surviving buildings in Northern Ireland. Built around 1690 using cruck-truss construction, the house was home to the Hezlett family for generations. Its simple whitewashed exterior and period furnishings offer a glimpse into centuries of rural domestic life.
shortDescription: A rare thatched 17th-century cruck-truss cottage near Castlerock, one of Northern Ireland's oldest buildings.

name: Springhill
description: Springhill is a charming 17th-century plantation house near Moneymore in County Londonderry, home to the Lenox-Conyngham family for ten generations. The whitewashed house contains a fine costume collection and a library of rare books. The grounds include a walled garden, beech-lined avenues, and the estate's original outbuildings.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century plantation house near Moneymore with a fine costume collection and walled garden.

name: Portstewart Strand
description: Portstewart Strand is a magnificent two-mile stretch of golden sandy beach backed by dunes on the north coast near Portstewart. The dune system is an important nature reserve supporting over 450 plant species including several rare orchids. The beach is popular for swimming and surfing, and vehicles can drive directly onto the sand during summer months.
shortDescription: A magnificent two-mile golden beach backed by species-rich dunes on the north coast.

pricing.label: Free parking
pricing.notes: There is no charge for parking

name: Roe Estuary
description: The Roe Estuary is a tranquil stretch of mudflats and salt marsh where the River Roe meets Lough Foyle in County Londonderry. It is an important wintering ground for migratory birds including whooper swans, pale-bellied brent geese, and bar-tailed godwits. The reserve offers birdwatching hides and walking paths along the estuary.
shortDescription: A tranquil estuary on Lough Foyle that is an important wintering ground for migratory birds.

name: The Argory
description: The Argory is a handsome 1820s Irish gentry house on a wooded hill above the River Blackwater in County Armagh. The house is lit by an original acetylene gas plant, one of very few surviving in the British Isles, and the interiors remain largely as the MacGeough-Bond family left them. The grounds include a riverside walk, pleasure garden, and rose garden.
shortDescription: An 1820s gentry house above the River Blackwater with original acetylene gas lighting and pleasure gardens.

name: Ardress House
description: Ardress House is a pretty 17th-century farmhouse in the apple orchard country of County Armagh, enlarged in Georgian times and featuring fine plasterwork by the celebrated stuccodore Michael Stapleton. The house contains a good collection of paintings and period furniture. The farmyard has been restored with traditional breeds of livestock and a display of old agricultural implements.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century Armagh farmhouse with fine Georgian plasterwork and a restored farmyard.

name: Derrymore House
description: Derrymore House is a thatched 18th-century cottage orne set in landscaped parkland near Newry in County Armagh. The elegant single-storey house, built around 1776, was where the Act of Union was drafted in 1800. The surrounding demesne features mature trees, a walled garden, and peaceful walks through rolling parkland.
shortDescription: A thatched 18th-century cottage orne near Newry where the Act of Union was drafted in 1800.

name: Coney Island, Lough Neagh
description: Coney Island is a small wooded island in the southern reaches of Lough Neagh, the largest freshwater lake in the British Isles. The island has evidence of habitation dating back thousands of years including a round tower foundation and early Christian remains. Accessible by boat from Maghery, it offers a unique retreat with bird-rich wetlands and ancient woodland.
shortDescription: A historic wooded island in Lough Neagh with early Christian remains and bird-rich wetlands.

name: Gray's Printing Press
description: Gray's Printing Press in Strabane is a small museum preserving an 18th-century print shop where John Dunlap, printer of the American Declaration of Independence, and James Wilson, grandfather of President Woodrow Wilson, learned their trade. The building houses original hand presses and typesetting equipment. It tells the remarkable story of Strabane's printing heritage and its links to America.
shortDescription: An 18th-century print shop in Strabane where the printer of the American Declaration of Independence learned his trade.

name: Wellbrook Beetling Mill
description: Wellbrook Beetling Mill is the last working linen beetling mill in Northern Ireland, set in a wooded glen near Cookstown in County Tyrone. The mill demonstrates the final stage of linen production where the cloth is pounded by water-driven hammers to produce its characteristic sheen. Guided tours explain the process and the importance of the linen industry to Ulster.
shortDescription: The last working linen beetling mill in Northern Ireland, demonstrating the traditional cloth-finishing process.

name: Minnowburn
description: Minnowburn is a popular riverside and woodland walk on the outskirts of south Belfast where the Minnowburn river meets the River Lagan. The area includes the Giant's Ring, a massive 5,000-year-old henge monument with a central dolmen. Mature woodland, wildflower meadows, and the towpath provide a peaceful escape from the city for walkers and cyclists.
shortDescription: A riverside walk on the edge of Belfast with access to the ancient Giant's Ring henge monument.

name: Lisnabreeny and Cregagh Glen
description: Lisnabreeny and Cregagh Glen is a green corridor of woodland and rath on the edge of east Belfast, offering walking trails through a wooded glen and open hillside with views over the city. The rath (ring fort) dates from the early Christian period and is a scheduled monument. The glen's stream, wildflowers, and birdsong make it a valued urban nature escape.
shortDescription: A wooded glen and ancient rath on the edge of east Belfast with panoramic city views.

name: Glenoe Waterfall
description: Glenoe Waterfall is a picturesque waterfall near the village of Gleno in County Antrim, cascading down a rocky gorge surrounded by mature woodland. A short walk from the village leads to the falls, which are particularly impressive after heavy rainfall. The surrounding countryside offers views across to the Scottish coast and is rich in wildflowers and birdlife.
shortDescription: A picturesque waterfall cascading through a wooded gorge near Gleno with views to Scotland.
